title: Introduction to Science
subtitle: Working scientifically: variables, data, graphs, errors and lab skills (Paper 2 skills).

studyGuide.heading: Working scientifically — the method
studyGuide.html:
Science is a way of investigating the world by testing ideas with experiments.

  • Ask a question and make a prediction (what you think will happen and why).
  • Plan a fair test — change one thing, measure one thing, keep everything else the same.
  • Collect results in a table, then display them on a graph.
  • Write a conclusion (what the results show) and an evaluation (how good the method was and how to improve it).

studyGuide.heading: Variables — the three you must know
studyGuide.html:
• Independent variable — the one you change (goes on the x-axis).
  • Dependent variable — the one you measure (goes on the y-axis).
  • Control variables — the ones you keep the same to make it a fair test.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.b{fill:#eaf1fe;stroke:#5b8def;stroke-width:2;rx:10}.t{font:bold 13px Nunito,sans-serif;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:11px Nunito,sans-serif;fill:#6b7790}</style><rect class='b' x='10' y='30' width='130' height='80' rx='10'/><text class='t' x='75' y='60' text-anchor='middle'>Independent</text><text class='s' x='75' y='80' text-anchor='middle'>you CHANGE it</text><text class='s' x='75' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(x-axis)</text><rect class='b' x='165' y='30' width='130' height='80' rx='10'/><text class='t' x='230' y='60' text-anchor='middle'>Dependent</text><text class='s' x='230' y='80' text-anchor='middle'>you MEASURE it</text><text class='s' x='230' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(y-axis)</text><rect class='b' x='320' y='30' width='130' height='80' rx='10'/><text class='t' x='385' y='60' text-anchor='middle'>Control</text><text class='s' x='385' y='80' text-anchor='middle'>keep it the SAME</text><text class='s' x='385' y='96' text-anchor='middle'>(fair test)</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Change one, measure one, keep the rest the same.

studyGuide.heading: Types of data → choosing a graph
studyGuide.html:
• Categoric data — values that are labels/words (e.g. type of metal, colour).
  • Continuous data — values that are numbers that can take any value (e.g. temperature, time, length).

Bar chart = one set of categoric data + one set of continuous data.
Line graph = two sets of continuous data (draw a line of best fit, do not join the dots).

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.ax{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:1.5}.lbl{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.sm{font:10px Nunito;fill:#6b7790}</style><line class='ax' x1='30' y1='20' x2='30' y2='130'/><line class='ax' x1='30' y1='130' x2='205' y2='130'/><rect x='45' y='80' width='25' height='50' fill='#5b8def'/><rect x='85' y='55' width='25' height='75' fill='#f0a35a'/><rect x='125' y='95' width='25' height='35' fill='#2b9d6b'/><rect x='165' y='40' width='25' height='90' fill='#e0524d'/><text class='lbl' x='115' y='152' text-anchor='middle'>Bar chart</text><text class='sm' x='115' y='165' text-anchor='middle'>categoric vs continuous</text><line class='ax' x1='265' y1='20' x2='265' y2='130'/><line class='ax' x1='265' y1='130' x2='440' y2='130'/><polyline points='275,120 305,105 335,82 365,60 395,42 425,30' fill='none' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='275' cy='120' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='305' cy='105' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='335' cy='82' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='365' cy='60' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='395' cy='42' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='425' cy='30' r='3' fill='#1f2a44'/><text class='lbl' x='350' y='152' text-anchor='middle'>Line graph</text><text class='sm' x='350' y='165' text-anchor='middle'>continuous vs continuous</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Bars for categories; a line of best fit for two number variables.

studyGuide.heading: Tables and units
studyGuide.html:
• The independent variable goes in the first (left) column; the dependent variable in the next column.
  • Units go in the heading only, not next to every number — e.g. Time (s), Volume of gas (cm³).
  • To find an average (mean): add the repeats and divide by how many — but ignore anomalous results first.

studyGuide.heading: Errors
studyGuide.html:
• Systematic error — a problem with the method or equipment (e.g. using a beaker instead of a measuring cylinder). Repeats do NOT fix it.
  • Random error — small differences every time you measure (e.g. reaction time on a stopwatch). Repeats and averaging reduce it.
  • Zero error — equipment doesn't read zero when it should (e.g. a balance). Reset it, or subtract the false reading.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Systematic = Same mistake every time (repeats don't help). Random = Reduced by Repeats.

studyGuide.heading: Key words examiners love
studyGuide.html:
• Accurate — close to the true value.
  • Precise — results are close together (small spread).
  • Anomalous — a result that doesn't fit the pattern (the odd one out).
  • Resolution — the smallest reading an instrument can measure.
  • Repeatable — same person, same method, gets similar results.
  • Reproducible — a different person/method gets similar results.
  • Range — the biggest and smallest values (e.g. 0–10 N).
studyGuide.mnemonic: Accurate = on the bullseye; Precise = arrows tightly grouped (even if off-target).

studyGuide.heading: Lab safety & equipment
studyGuide.html:
• Wear safety goggles; tie back hair; never taste chemicals; stand up and tuck stools in.
  • The Bunsen burner: yellow (safety) flame = air hole closed, cool and visible; blue (roaring) flame = air hole open, hot, used for heating.
  • Measure liquid volume with a measuring cylinder, reading at the bottom of the meniscus at eye level.

flashcards.back: The variable you change. Goes on the x-axis.
The variable you measure. Goes on the y-axis.
A variable kept the same to make it a fair test.
Data that is labels/words (e.g. type of plant). Shown on a bar chart.
Numerical data that can take any value (e.g. temperature). Shown on a line graph.
Results are close together — small spread.
A result that does not fit the pattern — the odd one out. Ignore it when averaging.
The smallest reading a piece of equipment can measure.
A fault in the method/equipment. Repeats do NOT reduce it.
Small variation each measurement. Reduced by repeats and averaging.
Equipment doesn't read zero when it should. Reset it or subtract the offset.
Repeatable: same person/method. Reproducible: different person/method, similar results.
The biggest and smallest values of a variable, e.g. 0–10 N.
A smooth line/curve through the middle of the points. Do NOT join the dots.
Yellow flame = air hole closed (cool, safety). Blue flame = air hole open (hot, for heating).

commonMistakes.title: Mixing up the axes
commonMistakes.detail: Independent (what you change) goes on the x-axis; dependent (what you measure) on the y-axis. Remember DRY MIX.

commonMistakes.title: Putting units next to every number
commonMistakes.detail: Units go in the table heading only, e.g. Time (s) — not '5s, 10s' in each cell.

commonMistakes.title: Joining the dots
commonMistakes.detail: On a line graph you draw a single line of best fit, not a zig-zag connecting every point.

commonMistakes.title: Saying repeats fix all errors
commonMistakes.detail: Repeats reduce random error but do NOT fix systematic or zero errors.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing accurate and precise
commonMistakes.detail: Accurate = close to true value. Precise = close together. You can be precise but not accurate.

commonMistakes.title: Vague conclusions
commonMistakes.detail: Don't write 'it went up'. Use the data: 'As temperature increased from 10 to 50°C, the rate increased'.

questions.q: Which variable do you change in an experiment?
questions.explanation: The independent variable is the one you deliberately change. It goes on the x-axis.
questions.hint: It's the cause, not the effect — think 'I change it'.

questions.q: On which axis do you plot the dependent variable?
questions.hint: Remember DRY MIX.

questions.q: Temperature measured in °C is an example of what type of data?
questions.explanation: Temperature is a number that can take any value, so it is continuous data.
questions.hint: Is it a word/label or a number?

questions.q: Which graph should you draw for two sets of continuous data?
questions.explanation: Two continuous variables → line graph with a line of best fit.
questions.hint: Bars are for categories.

questions.q: A result that does not fit the pattern is called…
questions.explanation: An anomalous result is the odd one out and should be ignored when averaging.
questions.hint: It's the 'odd one out'.

questions.q: Where should units go in a results table?
questions.explanation: Units go in the heading, e.g. Time (s). Numbers in the cells have no units.
questions.hint: Think about how a tidy table looks.

questions.q: Which Bunsen burner flame is the hot flame used for heating?
questions.explanation: Blue roaring flame (air hole open) is hot; yellow is the cool safety flame.
questions.hint: Open the air hole to get it hotter.

questions.q: 'Accurate' means a result is…
questions.explanation: Accurate = close to the true/real value.
questions.hint: Bullseye.

questions.q: State what a control variable is and why it matters.
questions.modelAnswer: A control variable is a variable that is kept the same throughout the experiment. It matters because it makes the test fair, so any change in the dependent variable is caused only by the independent variable.
questions.hint: Think 'kept the same' and 'fair test'.
questions.examMistake: Don't just say 'the thing you keep the same' — also say WHY (fair test).

questions.q: Name the piece of equipment you should use to measure exactly 25 cm³ of water, and how you read it.
questions.modelAnswer: A measuring cylinder. Read the volume at the bottom of the meniscus with your eye level with the liquid.
questions.hint: Not a beaker — and where do you look?
questions.examMistake: A beaker is not accurate enough; that would be a systematic error.

questions.q: Which is a categoric variable?
questions.explanation: Type of fruit is a label/word, so it is categoric.
questions.hint: Which one is a word, not a number?

questions.q: What is the 'range' of these values: 3, 5, 8, 11 N?
questions.explanation: Range is the smallest to the largest value: 3–11 N.
questions.hint: Smallest and biggest.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a random error and a systematic error, and how each can be reduced.
questions.modelAnswer: A random error causes small differences each time you measure (e.g. timing with a stopwatch); it is reduced by repeating readings and taking a mean. A systematic error is a fault in the method or equipment that shifts every reading the same way (e.g. using a beaker not a measuring cylinder); repeats do not fix it — you must change the method or equipment.
questions.hint: One is fixed by repeats, the other isn't.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'repeat the experiment' fixes systematic errors — it does not.

questions.q: A student gets 4.0, 4.1, 4.0, 7.8, 4.1 g. Which is the anomalous result and what should they do?
options: 7.8 g — ignore it when calculating the mean
questions.explanation: 7.8 g doesn't fit the pattern; ignore it before averaging the rest.
questions.hint: Which value doesn't fit the others?

questions.q: A graph of distance against time has points that rise steadily. Describe how to draw the trend correctly.
questions.modelAnswer: Draw a single straight line of best fit that goes through the middle of the points, with roughly the same number of points above and below. Do not join the dots and ignore any anomalous point.
questions.hint: Best fit — not dot-to-dot.
questions.examMistake: Joining each point with a zig-zag line loses marks.

questions.q: Results are very close together but all far from the true value. They are…
questions.explanation: Close together = precise; far from true value = not accurate.
questions.hint: Tightly grouped arrows, but off the bullseye.

questions.q: Define resolution and give an example of choosing equipment with better resolution.
questions.modelAnswer: Resolution is the smallest change an instrument can measure. For example, a ruler marked in mm has better resolution than one marked only in cm, so it gives more detailed readings.
questions.hint: Smallest reading possible.
questions.examMistake: Don't confuse resolution with accuracy — they are different ideas.

questions.q: Why do scientists repeat readings and calculate a mean?
options: To reduce the effect of random error and spot anomalies
questions.explanation: Repeats reduce random error and help identify anomalous results.
questions.hint: What kind of error do repeats help with?

questions.q: Write a clear conclusion for: 'As the temperature of water increased, sugar dissolved faster.' Use the idea of variables.
questions.modelAnswer: As the independent variable (temperature) increased, the dependent variable (rate of dissolving) increased — the sugar dissolved faster at higher temperatures. This shows a positive relationship between temperature and dissolving rate.
questions.hint: Name both variables and the direction of the trend.
questions.examMistake: Vague answers like 'it went up' don't reference the data or variables.

questions.q: A balance reads 2 g when nothing is on it. This is an example of…
questions.explanation: Equipment not reading zero when empty is a zero error.
questions.hint: What should it read when empty?

questions.q: Explain the difference between 'repeatable' and 'reproducible'.
questions.modelAnswer: Repeatable means the same person gets similar results when they repeat the experiment with the same method and equipment. Reproducible means a different person, or a different method, gets similar results. Reproducible results give more confidence the finding is real.
questions.hint: Same person vs different person.
questions.examMistake: These two words are often swapped — keep them straight.

questions.q: Which set-up gives the most valid (fair) test of how light affects plant growth?
options: Different plants, different pots, different water
Same plant type, same water and soil, only light changed
Measure a different plant each day
questions.explanation: Only the independent variable (light) should change; everything else is controlled.
questions.hint: Change one thing only.

questions.q: A student investigates how the length of a wire affects its resistance. Identify the independent, dependent and two control variables, and state one way to make the results more reliable.
questions.modelAnswer: Independent variable: length of wire. Dependent variable: resistance (or current for a fixed voltage). Control variables: type/material of wire, cross-sectional area (thickness) of wire, and the voltage/temperature. To make results more reliable, repeat each length several times and calculate a mean, ignoring anomalies.
questions.hint: What is changed, what is measured, and what must stay the same?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that wire thickness and material must be controlled.

questions.q: Explain why taking repeat readings improves the mean but cannot remove a systematic error, using an example.
questions.modelAnswer: Repeats let you average out random errors that scatter above and below the true value, so the mean is closer to the true value. A systematic error shifts every reading by the same amount in the same direction (e.g. a stopwatch always started 0.5 s late, or a balance with a zero error), so the average is still wrong by that amount. Repeating does not detect or remove it — you must fix the method or equipment.
questions.hint: Random errors cancel out; a constant shift does not.

questions.q: Two students measure the same length: Student A gets 5.0, 5.1, 4.9 cm; Student B gets 4.0, 6.0, 5.0 cm. True value is 5.0 cm. Which statement is correct?
options: A is more precise; both have the same mean
B is more precise than A
A is less accurate than B
questions.explanation: A's results are close together (precise) and both means are ~5.0 (accurate). B's are spread out (less precise).
questions.hint: Look at the spread of each set.

questions.q: A line graph of results shows a curve that levels off. Describe what this shape tells you and how you would read off a value accurately.
questions.modelAnswer: The curve levelling off shows the dependent variable increases at first but then changes less and less until it stops increasing (it reaches a maximum/plateau). To read a value, find the point on the x-axis, draw a vertical line up to the curve, then a horizontal line across to the y-axis, reading at eye level using the line of best fit (not individual points).
questions.hint: What does 'flattening' mean, and how do you read between points?
questions.examMistake: Reading off a scattered point instead of the line of best fit.

questions.q: Suggest why using a beaker instead of a measuring cylinder to measure 50 cm³ of water is a problem, and classify the error.
questions.modelAnswer: A beaker's markings are wide apart and approximate, so the volume measured is consistently inaccurate — it has poor resolution and introduces a systematic error because every measurement is off in a similar way. Using a measuring cylinder (better resolution) gives a more accurate, repeatable volume.
questions.hint: Think about both resolution and the type of error.
questions.examMistake: Calling it a random error — it's systematic.

questions.q: Which improvement would best increase the accuracy (not just precision) of a timing experiment using a stopwatch?
options: Use light gates instead of a hand-held stopwatch
Record more decimal places by guessing
questions.explanation: Light gates remove human reaction-time error (a systematic/random human error), improving accuracy. Repeats only reduce random error.
questions.hint: What causes the timing error in the first place?

questions.q: Define 'valid'. Explain how a poorly controlled variable can make results invalid even if they are precise.
questions.modelAnswer: Valid means the experiment actually tests what it claims to and the conclusion is supported because only the independent variable was changed. If a control variable is not kept constant, the dependent variable may change because of that uncontrolled factor instead of the independent variable, so the conclusion is not justified — the results can still be precise (close together) but invalid because the test was not fair.
questions.hint: Precise and valid are different things.
questions.examMistake: Assuming precise results are automatically valid.

questions.q: Plan, in outline, a fair-test investigation into how the length of time a gummy bear is soaked in water affects its length. Include the variables and how you would make it reliable.
questions.modelAnswer: Independent variable: time soaked (e.g. 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 minutes). Dependent variable: length of the gummy bear in mm. Control variables: same starting size/type of gummy bear, same volume and temperature of water, same way of measuring length. Method: measure starting length, soak for the set time, remove, dry gently and measure again. Repeat each time at least three times and take a mean, ignoring anomalies, then plot length against time as a line graph and describe the trend.
questions.hint: This is the gummy-bear practical — name variables, repeats, and the graph.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to control water volume/temperature, or not repeating for a mean.
